caveats: a: The occupation mix is four-digit rather than six-digit.
a: AI task exposure is inferred.
a: The supplied compensation ratio has a wage/receipts vintage mismatch and may vary sharply between handheld-instrument and semiconductor-test firms.
rho: NIST evidence describes manufacturing and metrology needs rather than realized labor substitution.
rho: Implementation barriers are much higher in semiconductor automated test equipment than in basic handheld instruments.
e: No public source measures lens eligibility.
e: NAICS is establishment-based and mixes basic meters with highly complex semiconductor test systems.
e: The supplied firm count uses a margin bridge that may poorly fit diverse product economics.
s5: The owner evidence is cross-industry, owner-level, and dated 2018.
s5: Age does not establish sale intent or transferability.
s5: Corporate carve-outs and internal transfers have different dynamics and are excluded unless they qualify.
q: No source directly measures AI-benefit retention.
q: Capture differs materially between commodity bench instruments and proprietary semiconductor test platforms.
d5: BLS data are four-digit.
d5: Government investment and metrology priorities do not directly measure industry purchases.
d5: Rapid performance improvement makes constant-quality adjustment difficult.
o: Value may migrate from instrument hardware to software, probes, or integrated semiconductor equipment.
o: The accountable operator may consolidate into larger platforms rather than disappear.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Instrument and system sales to electronics and semiconductor manufacturers, utilities, laboratories, OEMs, distributors, field technicians, and industrial users, with repeat demand from technology upgrades, fleet replacement, calibration, accessories, software, service contracts, and support.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal laboratories or plants, shells, financing vehicles, firms outside the EBITDA band, communications test equipment classified elsewhere, pure calibration services or software publishers classified elsewhere, and one-off operations without repeat external revenue.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of instruments for measuring and testing electricity and electrical signals that repeatedly serve external customers, including circuit testers, multimeters, wattmeters, signal and semiconductor test equipment, related software, calibration, configuration, and support.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation shares for four-digit NAICS 334500, not wage-weighted AI task exposure for 334515. I map its measured mix—17.58% architecture and engineering, 26.89% production, and 7.77% office support, plus management, computing, and sales—to high exposure in software assistance, test-program creation, documentation, application support, quotation, planning, and data analysis and low exposure in assembly, precision calibration, hardware debugging, environmental testing, and accountable metrology. The broader industry may differ in engineering and production intensity, producing two-sided error.
caveats: The occupation mix is four-digit rather than six-digit.
AI task exposure is inferred.
The supplied compensation ratio has a wage/receipts vintage mismatch and may vary sharply between handheld-instrument and semiconductor-test firms.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit OEWS staffing table plus wage-weighted task studies across R&D, test-program development, applications, calibration, production, quality, sales, and support would materially change exposure.
a.rationale: Electrical-test makers combine engineering, firmware, test software, technical documentation, application support, quality analysis, and commercial workflows where AI can reduce labor, but the instruments themselves require precision hardware, calibration, validation, and physical production.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for four-digit NAICS 334500 at a 2.1% annual compound rate from 2024 to 2034, equivalent to about 1.11 over five years. I raise the 334515 base modestly because NIST reports intensifying semiconductor metrology challenges and measurement involvement in many manufacturing steps, while DOE identifies higher-fidelity grid sensing and monitoring needs. These are adjacent demand drivers rather than a direct shipment forecast; semiconductors, grid tools, handheld meters, and other signal-test markets can diverge.
caveats: BLS data are four-digit.
Government investment and metrology priorities do not directly measure industry purchases.
Rapid performance improvement makes constant-quality adjustment difficult.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price US shipment and order forecasts split among semiconductor automated test, general electronic test, grid and power measurement, field instruments, and service/software would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Domestic semiconductor investment, more complex chips, electrification, grid modernization, electronic content, and faster standards support real growth in test demand. Semiconductor capital cycles, export controls, instrument reuse, software-defined substitution, and offshore equipment supply create downside.

caveats: No public source measures lens eligibility.
NAICS is establishment-based and mixes basic meters with highly complex semiconductor test systems.
The supplied firm count uses a margin bridge that may poorly fit diverse product economics.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level diligence across the supplied LMM population showing independent ownership, external repeat revenue, installed-base service and software mix, customer concentration, and standalone IP and systems would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Repeat instrument sales, installed-base software, calibration, accessories, and service can satisfy the external recurring lens. Eligibility is reduced by project-only custom equipment, captive or strategic subsidiaries, commodity device makers with limited service, customer-concentrated semiconductor suppliers, and firms that lack standalone transferable engineering and support systems.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 334515 ($5,055,190,236) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 334515 ($14,256,485,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2532 (margins.json[334]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Computers/Peripherals EBITDA/Sales 25.32%); revenue/employee $437,369 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 334515); class <5: 211 firms, avg revenue/firm $526,701 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $133,361 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 89 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,726,775 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $437,220 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 97 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,840,959 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $972,531 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 142 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,285,761 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,110,755 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 38 firms, avg revenue/firm $33,950,289 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,596,213 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $216,008,286 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $54,693,298 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 180

caveats: Value may migrate from instrument hardware to software, probes, or integrated semiconductor equipment.
The accountable operator may consolidate into larger platforms rather than disappear.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and product-line shipment evidence quantifying functions absorbed by embedded self-test, virtual instruments, commodity acquisition hardware, and software versus functions still requiring specialist calibrated equipment would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Semiconductor production, grid operation, product development, maintenance, and safety still require accountable suppliers of calibrated physical interfaces, probes, signal generation, measurement hardware, fixtures, and validated systems. Software-defined instruments, embedded self-test, cloud analytics, and commodity data acquisition can eliminate some standalone boxes but not the physical metrology boundary.

caveats: No source directly measures AI-benefit retention.
Capture differs materially between commodity bench instruments and proprietary semiconductor test platforms.
q.change_evidence: Contracts and order-level histories showing price revisions, service and software attachment, cost-down clauses, gross-margin persistence, competitive win/loss outcomes, and installed-base switching behavior would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Accuracy, calibration, application expertise, proprietary software, installed test programs, validation records, and customer switching costs can preserve AI-enabled savings. Competitive catalogs, distributors, large OEM procurement, semiconductor capital-equipment negotiations, and rapid feature imitation pass some benefit through over five years.

caveats: NIST evidence describes manufacturing and metrology needs rather than realized labor substitution.
Implementation barriers are much higher in semiconductor automated test equipment than in basic handheld instruments.
rho.change_evidence: Audited AI deployments at small and midsize electrical-test makers showing sustained workflow adoption, labor-hour reduction, error rates, calibration impact, and qualification burden would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Within five years, coding copilots, automated documentation, application-support agents, proposal automation, planning tools, and AI-assisted defect analysis should be deployable. Metrology traceability, proprietary data, semiconductor customer qualification, cybersecurity, high equipment complexity, reliability, and human approval constrain realized implementation.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of US employer businesses across industries were age 55 or older in 2018; it does not measure 334515 transfers. I discount the age pool for corporate and sponsor ownership, multiple owners, continued operation, family succession, closures, failed deals, internal reorganizations, and technical businesses that cannot transfer. The proxy may overstate transfer probability if strategic subsidiaries are common.
caveats: The owner evidence is cross-industry, owner-level, and dated 2018.
Age does not establish sale intent or transferability.
Corporate carve-outs and internal transfers have different dynamics and are excluded unless they qualify.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, or Axial deal histories mapped to 334515 together with an owner-age and succession survey of independent test-instrument manufacturers would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Owner aging creates plausible succession supply, but only a minority of eligible firms are likely to undergo an external qualifying control transfer within five years.

principal_driver: Rising semiconductor and electrification complexity sustains calibrated electrical-test demand and repeat installed-base workflows.
principal_weakness: Product heterogeneity and software-defined substitution make six-digit demand and eligible-firm economics difficult to measure.

run_meta.title: Instrument Manufacturing for Measuring and Testing Electricity and Electrical Signals

sources.supports: Defines 334515 and examples including circuit and continuity testers, voltmeters, ohm meters, wattmeters, multimeters, and semiconductor test equipment.

sources.supports: Projects NAICS 334500 real output from $149.7 billion in 2024 to $184.7 billion in 2034, a 2.1% annual compound rate.
sources.title: Employment and output by industry, 2024–34

sources.supports: Reports the broader-industry occupation mix, including 17.58% architecture and engineering, 26.89% production, and 7.77% office support employment.

sources.supports: Describes manufacturing AI use cases including generative design, predictive maintenance, and quality improvement while noting adoption barriers.
sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in U.S. Manufacturing

sources.supports: States that semiconductor metrology challenges intensify as devices become smaller, more complex, and multilayered, affecting yield, quality, and cost.
sources.title: CHIPS for America Metrology Program

sources.supports: Reports that measurement science plays a role in up to 50% of semiconductor manufacturing steps and supports high-quality mass production.
sources.title: Why NIST Is Putting Its CHIPS Into U.S. Manufacturing

sources.supports: Explains the need for higher-fidelity grid measurement, lower-cost multimodal sensing, reduced uncertainty, broad coverage, and resilient communications.
sources.title: GMLC 1.2.5 - Sensing and Measurement Strategy

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
